title: Rajesh Kumar Sharma v. Infosys BPO Limited & Anr.
court: High Court of Karnataka at Bangalore
keyArguments: The termination of the petitioner was effected without following the principles of natural justice as no show cause notice was issued prior to dismissal
The respondent company failed to conduct a proper domestic inquiry as mandated under the Standing Orders certified under the Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act, 1946
The alleged misconduct of the petitioner was not supported by any cogent or reliable evidence on record and the findings of the inquiry officer were perverse
The petitioner had an unblemished service record of eleven years and the punishment of dismissal was disproportionate to the alleged misconduct
The termination letter lacked specific charges and was vague, thereby violating the petitioner's right to a fair hearing
judgmentSummary: The High Court of Karnataka allowed the writ petition filed by the petitioner-employee challenging his termination from service. The Court held that the dismissal was illegal and void ab initio on account of non-compliance with principles of natural justice and procedural requirements under the certified Standing Orders. The respondent company was directed to reinstate the petitioner with full back wages and continuity of service.
judgmentText:
IN THE HIGH COURT OF KARNATAKA AT BANGALORE

WRIT PETITION NO. 14872 OF 2022

BETWEEN:
Rajesh Kumar Sharma, S/o Late Mohan Lal Sharma, Aged about 38 years, Residing at No. 14, 3rd Cross, Vijayanagar, Bangalore - 560040. ... PETITIONER

AND:
1. Infosys BPO Limited, Represented by its Managing Director, Electronics City, Hosur Road, Bangalore - 560100.
2. The Deputy Labour Commissioner, Bangalore Urban District, Shram Shakti Bhavan, Bangalore - 560001. ... RESPONDENTS

DATE OF JUDGMENT: 17th March, 2023

HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE VENKATESH NARAYANA REDDY

JUDGMENT

The petitioner has approached this Court under Article 226 and 227 of the Constitution of India challenging the order of termination dated 03rd August, 2021 passed by the first respondent company whereby the petitioner was dismissed from service on allegations of unauthorized disclosure of confidential client data.

Facts in Brief:

The petitioner was employed as a Senior Process Associate with the first respondent since the year 2010. By the impugned order, his services were terminated with immediate effect. It is the case of the petitioner that he was not served with any show cause notice and no domestic inquiry was conducted prior to his dismissal. The termination letter merely stated that the petitioner had violated the company's data security policy without specifying the nature, date, or particulars of the alleged breach.

Hearing and Submissions:

Learned counsel for the petitioner Sri. Aditya Ranganath submitted that the dismissal is wholly illegal as it violates the mandatory requirements of the certified Standing Orders of the respondent company and the principles of natural justice. Reliance was placed on the decisions of the Hon'ble Supreme Court in Workmen of Firestone Tyre and Rubber Co. of India Pvt. Ltd. v. Management (1973) and Bharat Forge Co. Ltd. v. Uttam Manohar Nakate (2005) 2 SCC 489.

Learned counsel for the first respondent Sri. Pradeep Venkataraman submitted that the petitioner had executed a Non-Disclosure Agreement and the matter was governed by the terms of the employment contract. It was further contended that the petitioner had admitted to the breach in an internal communication.

Analysis and Findings:

This Court has carefully examined the material on record. It is an established principle of service jurisprudence that an employee cannot be dismissed from service without being given a reasonable opportunity to be heard. The Standing Orders applicable to the first respondent company, which are certified under the Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act, 1946, clearly mandate that no order of dismissal shall be passed without holding a proper domestic inquiry.

A perusal of the record reveals that no inquiry proceedings were initiated against the petitioner. The so-called internal communication relied upon by the respondent was never put to the petitioner for his explanation. The termination order is bereft of any specific particulars regarding the alleged misconduct.

This Court is of the considered view that the termination of the petitioner is vitiated by non-compliance with the principles of natural justice and the mandatory procedural safeguards. The reliance placed by the respondent on the employment contract cannot override statutory provisions. The petitioner's eleven years of unblemished service further supports a finding that the extreme punishment of dismissal was wholly disproportionate.

Order:

In view of the foregoing, the Writ Petition is allowed. The impugned order of termination dated 03rd August, 2021 is hereby quashed and set aside. The first respondent is directed to reinstate the petitioner to his original post with full back wages and continuity of service within a period of four weeks from the date of this order. No costs.

Sd/-
HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE VENKATESH NARAYANA REDDY
